TextENGNew: The shop where Dad had ordered his jackets was a stylish little boutique.
There was no way Dad would have walked into a place like this on his own.
...Rina-san must have brought him here. That much was obvious.
When I handed over the slip, the store manager came out from the back wearing an apologetic smile.
``Ryuugu-sama, I'm terribly sorry.@k@vS09/00/regiA17002. It seems the delivery truck got caught in accident traffic on the highway...@k@vS09/00/regiA17003. It should be arriving shortly, though...``
He said the driver had called after getting off at such-and-such interchange, so it would be about another hour. A simple enough matter, but he explained it with excessive roundaboutness.
When I asked if I should come back another day,@k he insisted the truck would be here any minute and offered me a seat on their reception sofa.
...But the shop's atmosphere was a bit much for me, so I told him I'd kill time outside and stepped out.
If there were a fast-food place nearby, that would be perfect.@k But I was pretty sure I'd have to go all the way back to the station for that.
Looking around, I spotted a coffee shop.
The door was black-tinted glass, so I couldn't see inside, which made me a little uneasy.
...But a sign in the window said women got a discount on tea on certain days.
...I was just going to kill a little time. It would be fine.
After the briefest hesitation, I put my hand on the glass door.
Her first impression upon stepping inside was the reek of cigarette smoke.
By definition, a coffee shop having wisps of smoke drifting about was nothing unusual.@k ...But Rena instantly regretted coming in.
She was debating whether to turn on her heel and leave when the brusque proprietor addressed her, and Rena gave up, letting herself be led to a seat.
The interior held none of the refined salon atmosphere one might expect of a place for enjoying coffee and cigarettes. It felt more like a hangout for rough, unsavory types.
She couldn't get comfortable with the atmosphere, and worst of all, the shrill laughter of a man and woman at the very back of the shop grated on her nerves.

Rena's seat happened to be behind a planter, which, depending on how you looked at it, put her in the spot most shielded from their laughter.@k ...She looked as though she wanted to finish her tea and leave immediately.
The clinking bell at the entrance announced another customer.
...It was a wonder a dingy coffee shop like this got any business at all.
Rena peered at the newcomers from behind the planter.
There were two of them.
...Rough-looking men, clearly bad news. One glance at their grim faces told you to stay well away.
When the proprietor moved to greet them, the two ignored him and headed straight for the back.
They approached the man and woman who'd been cackling away, bowing their heads deferentially.
``Hey!@k@vS09/21/172000002. 'Bout time ya showed up.@k@vS09/21/172000003. Siddown.``
Upon noticing their visitors had arrived, the couple stopped their raucous laughing, and the man told the newcomers to sit in a voice heavy with menace.
The two men meekly took their seats as directed.
``W-We're sorry, we tried to...``
``Spare me the excuses, dipshit.@k@vS09/21/172000007. Hand over the goods.``
The two men glanced at each other, then each produced a worn envelope from inside their jackets and placed them on the table.
``Check it.``
``Hey, don't order me around.@k@vS09/20/rina07002. It was my money to begin with.``
``Shaddap!!@k@vS09/21/teppe07003. Just count the damn thing...``
The man barked at her. The woman licked her thumb with an annoyed look, then began flipping through the bills one by one.
The two visitors sat with their heads bowed, watching.
The man lit a fresh cigarette and seemed content to wait until the woman finished her count.
``Yep, it's all here.``
The two visitors visibly relaxed.
``...Ritsuko.@k@vS09/21/teppe07005. That everything?``
``I just said it's all here.``
``I'm askin' if that's everything including my cut!!``
``And I'm telling you, the amount I specified is all here!``
``Dumbass!!@k@vS09/21/teppe07008. I'm askin' if my share's in there too!!``
Despite this volatile exchange happening at full volume, the other customers in the shop acted as though they heard nothing.
``Hey, boys.@k@vS09/21/teppe07010. This ain't what we agreed on.@k@vS09/21/teppe07011. What's the deal?``
``Tecchan.@k@vS09/20/rina07006. Don't bully them, that's not nice.@k@vS09/20/rina07007. Sorry about him, boys. My man here has a short fuse.``
``Short fuse my ass!!@k@vS09/21/teppe07013. I'm fired up 'cause these punks are messin' with you!``
``Oh, stop it, being stupid won't get you anywhere.@k@vS09/20/rina07009. ...Mmm, hehe...!``
The two resumed their braying laughter, just as they'd been doing before the visitors arrived.
Their laughter was clearly meant to intimidate the two men.

``Listen, you two.@k@vS09/20/rina07011. Tecchan happens to be in a really good mood today.@k@vS09/20/rina07012. On a normal day, you'd have been stripped naked and thrown in the Tamago River by now.``
``W-We're so sorry... so sorry...``
``Well, there's no point in being too hard on them.@k@vS09/20/rina07014. Let's get to the real business.``
The men seemed eager to get it over with. They took the pens offered to them and hurriedly filled out something on the documents spread across the table.
Watching them, the man and Ritsuko exchanged satisfied smirks—things had gone exactly as planned.
The documents apparently had many fields to fill in, and the two men took a while.
The man and Ritsuko, knowing it would take some time, cheerfully began chatting about vulgar topics.
``Ritsuko, how's that husband of yours in Hinamizawa doin'?``
``Hmm?@k@vS09/20/172100064. Don't call him my husband.``
``How's it goin' with him?@k@vS09/21/172000048. I heard he's sittin' on a pile o' cash.@k@vS09/21/172000049. How much d'ya think you can squeeze out?``
``Oh, you won't believe it!@k@vS09/20/172100066. I'd heard his ex-wife gave him a big settlement in the divorce.@k@vS09/20/172100067. But it turns out... it's way more than I thought!``
``How much are we talkin'?``
``About fifty million yen sitting in the bank.@k@vS09/20/172100069. It's easy money to him, so he throws it around like nothing.``
``Hot damn!@k@vS09/21/172000052. That's huge!!@k@vS09/21/172000053. So how far d'ya think ya can take it...?``

``He's absolutely smitten with me.@k@vS09/20/rina07016. He buys me anything I want. Anything!@k@vS09/20/rina07017. Nothing like a cheapskate like you~@k@vS09/20/rina07018. Maybe I should get serious about him.``
``In yer dreams.@k@vS09/21/teppe07015. No other man could satisfy ya like I can!``
``Mmm...!@k@vS09/20/rina07020. ...Oh stop, not here, come on...``
``So, what's yer play with the Hinamizawa husband?``
changeReason: Old asks generic "what's the plan?"; new adds specific target "what's yer play with the Hinamizawa husband?" matching Japanese.

TextENGNew: ``Well... *whisper whisper*``

``Gwahahahaha!!@k@vS09/21/172000059. Yer one scary woman, ya know that?!@k@vS09/21/172000060. Call me when ya need someone for the stage show.@k@vS09/21/172000061. I'll play the angry boyfriend real good.``
``...Um... excuse us. ...We're finished.``
``Right!@k@vS09/21/teppe07017. Then let's get movin'.@k@vS09/21/teppe07018. Ritsuko, handle the tab.``
The man threw an arm around the two visitors' shoulders and opened the door to leave.
...Just then, someone was apparently trying to enter, and they nearly collided at the doorway.
Given the man's rough nature, it was hard to imagine he'd yield the way.
...But when he saw who stood on the other side, he stepped aside.
The man who entered looked every bit as rough as the others.
...Black suit, sunglasses.
His build alone wasn't particularly imposing, but the severity of his expression and his sheer presence were formidable.
changeReason: New adds "sheer presence" detail matching Japanese 威圧感 that old simplified.

TextENGNew: If the first man was a street thug, this newcomer was clearly a professional.
Even the man who'd been swaggering about so brazenly seemed to think better of it, his manner turning cautious.
When Ritsuko finished paying the tab and came over, she noticed the man in the suit.
``Oh... General Manager...?@k@vS09/20/172100079. G-Good morning, sir.``
The man in the suit—called the General Manager—said nothing. He merely jerked his chin upward, as if to say: get lost.
Ritsuko and the others bowed their heads and hurried out of the shop.
The proprietor also noticed the suited man and greeted him obsequiously, wringing his hands.
...Was he some sort of local power broker?

He carried a quieter air than the others, but if he could make even them step aside, he had to be a fearsome figure.@k ...Everyone in the shop seemed to share that thought, carefully avoiding eye contact.
The suited man surveyed the interior with a sharp gaze, then looked up at the menu board on the wall.
Thinking something might be wrong, the proprietor approached him nervously...
``...Waiter.``
``Y-Yes sir. Is... is there anything troubling you, sir...?``
``...This daily dessert special... the, uh... sw... swee... sweet-something-or-other. Do you have it today?``
``Huh?@k@vS09/00/mas17003. Oh... ahahaha, well, actually, we aren't running that special today, but... if you insist, we could prepare it specially for you...``
``...If you're not serving it, you're not serving it.@k@vS09/15/1715000005. I'll come back another day.``
``Come on, Kasai!@k@vS09/06/170600019. He just said he'd make it specially for you!@k@vS09/06/170600020. After we came all this way, don't be so embarrassing!``
Apparently unable to read the heavy, oppressive atmosphere of the shop, in came a girl laughing cheerfully—it was Mion's twin sister, Shion.
Shion pushed the reluctant Kasai inside and strode right in.
Then she spotted a girl sitting at one of the tables, and stopped.
``I knew it! Hello!@k@vS09/06/170600023. It's Rena-san, isn't it!@k@vS09/06/170600024. Hi there!``
``What a surprise, running into you here!``
``Hm?@k@vS09/06/170600027. You're alone?@k@vS09/06/170600028. Waiting for someone?@k@vS09/06/170600029. My sis, maybe?@k@vS09/06/170600030. Or could it be Kei-chan~?``
``Um... Shii-chan.@k@vS09/02/170200276. ...Who is that with you...?``
``Hm?@k@vS09/06/170600032. You mean Kasai?``
``Think of him as my bodyguard.@k@vS09/06/170600034. When my life's in danger, Kasai will protect me, right~?``
``...If that is your wish, even at the cost of my life.``
``Wow~...@k@vS09/06/170600036. Kasai, if you were twenty years younger, that might actually be dangerous.``
``He looks scary, but he's actually a great guy.@k@vS09/06/170600038. And this is Rena Ryuugu-san.@k@vS09/06/170600039. She's one of my sister's friends.``
``...Kasai.@k@vS09/15/1715000008. Pleased to meet you.``
``Um... K-... Kasai-san.@k@vS09/02/170200278. Did you know the people who just passed you?``
More or less.@k Kasai said with a small nod.
``Who?@k@vS09/06/170600041. I've never seen any of them before.``
``...The man is Teppei Houjou.@k@vS09/15/1715000010. A rude, worthless scoundrel.``
At the name Houjou, Shion let out a small "ah, that..." under her breath.
``...And the woman...?``
``...Rina Mamiya.@k@vS09/15/1715000012. That's her stage name, I believe.@k@vS09/15/1715000013. Her real name is Ritsuko, if I recall. She works as a hostess at one of the clubs on Flower Road.``
``Kasai-san.@k@vS09/02/170200281. ...What kind of relationship do Rina-san and that Houjou have?@k@vS09/02/170200282. They looked very close.``
``Houjou and Rina are... intimately involved, I believe.@k@vS09/15/1715000015. They're extremely bad news. I'd advise you not to get entangled with them.``
``How bad?``
Rena pressed with unexpected intensity, and Kasai seemed slightly taken aback.
``...A gentleman doesn't pry into a lady's private affairs.``
``How bad are they?``
``...I'd rather not say.``
``How bad are they?``
Rena repeated the exact same question, again and again, until she got her answer.
Alarmed by her desperation, Shion intervened.
``Kasai~.@k@vS09/06/170600043. Don't be mean—just tell her.@k@vS09/06/170600044. She's obviously not asking out of idle curiosity.@k@vS09/06/170600045. Right, Rena-san?``
A taut tension stretched between Rena and Kasai.@k Shion alone seemed oblivious to it.
Eventually, Kasai relented and opened his mouth.
``Those two are the worst sort of lowlifes.@k@vS09/15/1715000021. I've heard they make their living through extortion and gambling.@k@vS09/15/1715000022. Fraud, too, apparently.``
``Fraud... what kind?``
``Ahaha, well, their fraud is basically—what would you call it?@k@vS09/06/170600047. A romance scam, I suppose?``
``In short, the woman seduces a man, gets him nice and attached, and then her partner storms in—'what are you doing with my woman!'—and shakes him down for money. A classic badger game.``
``...They bleed the target dry with gifts and spending, then move in to take everything that's left.@k@vS09/15/1715000024. ...Word is she recently hooked a big catch.``
``Yikes...@k@vS09/06/170600050. Whoever that poor guy is, I feel sorry for him.``
``Then again, he did let himself get led around by the nose.@k@vS09/06/170600052. You reap what you sow, I guess?``
``...That's a bit harsh, Shion-san.@k@vS09/15/1715000026. A man can sometimes fall for a woman before he even knows what hit him.``

``Ohh, really?@k@vS09/06/170600054. So who's the woman that swept you off your feet, Kasai?@k@vS09/06/170600055. Waiter, the dessert please—for me, Kasai, and Rena-san too—... wait, huh? Where did she go?``
``She just left.``
Rena was already gone. Only the glass door, still swinging shut, seemed to know where she had gone...
When I got home, the door was locked.
I let myself in with my key and found a note left in the living room.
It read: "Something came up, so I'm going out. I'll eat dinner out tonight."
This kind of thing wasn't unusual lately.
Dad would only ever tell me he had to run to Okinomiya for something.
But I knew the truth, because I'd overheard him on the phone.
Rina-san must have had a sudden opening in her work schedule and invited him out to dinner.
I let out a quiet sigh, looked at the living room I couldn't get used to, and retreated to my room.
The conversation I'd just overheard at the coffee shop replayed vividly in my mind.
...I tried to interpret it charitably—maybe Rina-san was mixed up with a dangerous man and had only been playing along to keep the peace.
But she'd maintained her domineering attitude over the two men the entire time, without a shred of sympathy or concern.
...If she'd truly just been putting on an act, it wouldn't have looked like that.
If it were truly just a front, at least a trace of compassion or guilt would have leaked through.
Like cupping water in your hands—
no matter how tightly you close them, droplets seep through.@k Sympathy and remorse seep through.
But from Rina-san, not a drop had escaped. Not one.
She wasn't just dry—she was bone-dry, through and through.
There was no water to leak in the first place.
At that moment, Rina-san and the man had clearly been conducting something criminal.
It wasn't a matter of one leading and the other following.
They were in it together.
...That was it.
Rina-san was... a "bad person."
My brain cells accepted the conclusion with a standing ovation.
...Because I'd always disliked Rina-san.
But because of her relationship with Dad, I'd never been able to accept that feeling.
And now... at this moment, I could finally accept it...
She was a "bad person," just like my mother.
Someone whose mere presence beside Dad would ruin everything—an existence that destroyed happiness simply by existing.
But even if I told Dad, would he understand that Rina-san was a bad person?
Dad was besotted with her to the marrow of his bones.
He interpreted her every word and gesture in the most flattering, favorable light imaginable.
He defended her unbidden, praised her unprompted.
...I'm not a child anymore.
I understand how a woman can wrap a man around her finger.
It's different from the love and affection of building a partnership.
It's nothing more than an extension of the desire to dominate—to subjugate the other person.
Men... even my own father...@k can be systematically ensnared using certain sordid methods, as if following a manual.
It's a vulnerability inherent in being born male.
No amount of willpower can resist it.
That's precisely why we despise women who exploit that weakness to ensnare men.@k That's precisely why I could never bring myself to like Rina-san.
For the sake of argument, I might tolerate such a one-sided relationship.@k ...But only if it were actually love.
...If it weren't even love—if it were merely a tool for extortion—@k that would be an absolutely unforgivable, utterly despicable act...
The conversation from the coffee shop echoed in my mind.
...That's right—Rina-san had called Dad a big spender.
And it was true: ever since Rina-san came into his life, Dad had been spending money recklessly.
I'd been glad that my despondent father was taking an interest in the world again... but now I wasn't sure that was cause for celebration.
Dad managed the family finances, but since I did the shopping, he often entrusted me with the bankbook to make withdrawals.
So I knew exactly where the important things—the bankbooks, the personal seal—were kept.
In a locked drawer of Dad's study.
...I knew where the key was hidden, and I knew the combination to the portable safe inside.
I imagined Dad might come home unexpectedly, and felt a jolt of nerves.@k ...But I had to know...
I opened the portable safe. Out spilled several bankbooks and personal seals, along with leftover stamps and unused postcards.
When I took everything out to reach the bankbooks, I froze.
...At the bottom of the safe lay a bundle of crisp ten-thousand-yen bills.
Its thickness was not something that could be dismissed.
There was also a torn paper band that had apparently been wrapped around it.@k The band bore a stamp indicating it had originally bundled one million yen.
Since going to the bank every time was a hassle, Dad always kept some cash on hand.
But that was normally in the range of 100,000 to 200,000 yen at most.
He'd never kept such a large sum—the kind that comes wrapped in a bank band—at home before.
...The sheer volume of ten-thousand-yen bills was, all by itself, screaming that something was wrong.
My fingers felt suddenly clumsy as I tried to open the savings bankbook.
I could feel something inside me rising up, trying to deny what I'd overheard at the coffee shop.
...Not because I wanted to defend Dad or Rina-san.
...Because I didn't want the "husband in Hinamizawa" those extortionists were targeting to be my own father...
I opened the bankbook.
I'd last looked at it a few months ago. Since that date, countless withdrawals had been recorded.
Normally, there should be nothing to read from these emotionless numbers.
...But to me, in that moment, those ten simple digits from 0 to 9 spoke with a devastating eloquence.
It was... a gruesome parade of numbers.
At first, the figures were reasonable—amounts that could be explained as dinner expenses or the like.
...But gradually, they shifted to bold, round numbers—fives, tens.
From the pattern of withdrawal dates, I could clearly sense Dad's mindset: keeping cash in his wallet at all times to fund his outings with Rina-san.
Mixed among those figures, a sudden large expense appeared.
...Hundreds of thousands of yen—far too much for a date.@k ...I checked the date and searched my memory.
...That's right... around that time... Rina-san had mentioned something about moving into a new apartment.
I recalled the going rate for rental apartments around Okinomiya.
...Two months' deposit, two months' key money.@k ...The amount was plausible.@k ...Dad had paid the entire up-front cost of Rina-san's new apartment.
After that, bold numbers paraded across the page as if celebrating a housewarming.
His withdrawals were growing increasingly careless.
changeReason: Old fabricates 'congratulatory gifts for new apartment'; Japanese says withdrawals were growing careless.

TextENGNew: The amounts shifted from "just what I need" to "let me grab a lump sum to have on hand."
changeReason: Old says "numbers got bigger"; new explains shift from "just what I need" to "grab a lump sum" -- much more specific.

TextENGNew: ...That transition meant only one thing.
changeReason: Old explains pattern at length; new reduces to "That transition meant only one thing" -- major information difference.

TextENGNew: The raw, vivid collapse of my father's sense of money.
As the balance steadily shrank and I began to fear what would happen next, a large deposit suddenly appeared.
...Where had such a sum come from?
Only one source came to mind.@k ...I opened the other bankbook, the one with a different-colored cover.
It was absurdly simple—like a puzzle from a children's magazine.
A jigsaw with pieces so oversized that you didn't even need to try fitting them together to see the whole picture.
...Dad had dipped into his fixed-term deposits—money meant for the future.
That money was the enormous settlement from the divorce with my mother.
...To him, it should have been cursed money.
I could understand wanting to spend it in the name of a new love.
But... that was just an excuse to break open the savings.
Money is money.
Divorce settlement or not, it was still vital savings for our future.
The fact that it was "cursed money" didn't make it okay to pour it out like water.
Expenses that couldn't be explained by dating costs alone began to multiply.
Six-figure expenditures started recurring with alarming frequency.
Something about those amounts evoked the prices of appliances and furniture.
Dad must have been buying Rina-san everything she asked for.
...If Rina-san's group had intended to exploit Dad from the start, they must have been testing how much they could squeeze out of him.
And since Dad had been buying expensive things without batting an eye, they must have pegged him as a prime target.@k The progression of numbers told that story clearly.
When I shifted my attention to the dates of those entries, the dry, clinical digits began to speak eloquently once more.
Right around that period, Rina-san had started visiting our house more frequently, and began staying overnight.
...To Rina-san, Dad had initially been nothing more than a free-spending man,@k but around that point, he became her mark.
Dad, too, had started referring to her by name—"Rina-san"—instead of "my friend in Okinomiya" around that same time.
Dad was irresponsible, I'll give you that...
But he'd been betrayed by the wife he loved, and his spirit had been ground down to a pitiable state.
...And part of the blame for that lay with me.@k So I didn't want to put it all on him.
Besides... Dad isn't exactly what you'd call a ladies' man.
He had no natural immunity to women.@k When an alluring woman aggressively approached him during his lowest point, there was no way he could have refused.
Dad was now utterly infatuated with Rina-san, blind to everything else.
That was only natural—Rina-san had engineered it to be so.
I returned the bankbooks and cash to their original arrangement and closed the safe.
...This safe was a mirror of Dad's heart.
Its contents being devoured by Rina-san, one piece at a time...!
...What should I do, what should I do.@k Think, Rena Ryuugu.
Tell Dad about Rina-san's scheme?
...No. It probably wouldn't accomplish anything.
The definition of livestock is that it won't flee even when the gate is open.
...Dad wouldn't leave his pen even if I threw the door wide.
Confront Rina-san in front of Dad?
...Same result.
Rina-san would cling to Dad for cover.
He'd leap to her defense.
Whisper a few sweet words in his ear, and a man will protect you all on his own.
...If it got reframed as a conflict between me and Dad, things would only get worse.
I was trying to drive Rina-san away for Dad's sake.@k Any rift between us would only play into Rina-san's hands.@k I'd be walking into her trap.
So... did that mean there was nothing I could do through Dad?
If I couldn't make Dad cut ties with Rina-san, then I would have to confront Rina-san directly.
...Yes—that meant not rousing Dad to fight on my behalf, but fighting this battle myself.
But how?
...Right, that man—Kasai-san, who'd been with Shii-chan—seemed to know a lot about Rina-san and that Teppei person.
Could I find a way to reach him again?
He'd been intimidating, but Shion had introduced me as Mii-chan's friend.
...In these parts, Mii-chan's name carried considerable weight.@k He wouldn't treat me badly...
What if I could get him to put pressure on Rina-san—let her know her scheme had been exposed—@k ...so she'd quietly vanish from Dad's life...
But I had no idea how to find Kasai-san,@k ...and I didn't want Mii-chan and the others knowing about my family's situation...
This was, after all, a Ryuugu family problem.
It had nothing to do with anyone else.
This was a battle I had to fight alone.
I'd already lived through the crushing regret of my parents' divorce.
changeReason: Old says "I regretted my parents' divorce"; new says "I'd already lived through the crushing regret" -- different temporal framing and intensity.

TextENGNew: ...I'd wept over a tragedy I might have prevented through my own efforts.
That was why... this time, I would not weep.
I would not let the chance to avert disaster slip away.
This time... I would fight for my happiness.
